title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
This page shows how the data available for the 2027 decision would be passed through each CMP. It also identifies where the approximately 630 kt estimate from the 2026 northern Chile acoustic survey could, and could not, be used.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
section: 1 First settle which data year applies
text:
1 First settle which data year applies
The current MSE runs use a one-year data delay and a one-year management delay. Under that tested timing:

the CMP calculation made in 2026 produces catch advice for 2027;
the most recent observations used are from 2025;
the three-year CMPs use 2023–2025 data; and
MP23 uses 2021–2025 data because its current comparison run has a five-year averaging window.

The 2026 northern Chile acoustic estimate therefore does not enter the 2027 calculation as it was tested in the MSE. It would first enter the following annual calculation, for 2028 advice.
Using observations from 2026 for the 2027 TAC would require a same-year data rule. That would change the timing assumed in the tested CMP and should be specified, coded, and checked before it is used. Under such a revised rule, the three-year window would be 2024–2026 and the MP23 window would be 2022–2026.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
text:
2 Data required
Six CMPs use all five indices. Two use the four CPUE and fishery indices and exclude the acoustic survey.








CMPs
Annual series required
Is the northern Chile acoustic survey used?




MP18, MP23, MP24, MP29, MP31, MP32
Northern Chile acoustic survey (Chile_AcousN), Chile CPUE, Peru artisanal index, Peru industrial index, and offshore CPUE
Yes, when its data year falls within the approved window


MP35, MP36
Chile CPUE, Peru artisanal index, Peru industrial index, and offshore CPUE
No



For every series, retain the value, unit, survey or fleet coverage, source, revision status, and date received. Values must be comparable with those used in the 2019–2023 reference period.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
section: 3 Turn the observations into the CMP indicator
text:
3 Turn the observations into the CMP indicator
The raw indices are on different scales, so they cannot be averaged directly. The current procedure performs the following calculation.

3.1 Express each series relative to its reference-period average
For index (j) in year (y),
\[
R_{j,y} =
\frac{\text{index }j\text{ in year }y}
{\text{mean of index }j\text{ over 2019--2023}}.
\]
A value of 1 means that the observation equals its own 2019–2023 average. A value of 1.25 means it is 25% above that average.
The reported acoustic estimate of about 630 kt cannot be entered directly into the harvest rule. If its year is eligible, it must first be checked against the same northern Chile acoustic series and converted to
\[
R_{\text{acoustic},2026} =
\frac{630\text{ kt}}
{\text{mean northern Chile acoustic biomass over 2019--2023}}.
\]
The reference-period mean is not recorded on this site, so this contribution cannot yet be calculated here.


3.2 Combine the available series within each year
For each year, average the relative values for the indices required by the CMP. This gives one combined annual index (A_y).
The analysis code averages the non-missing series. Before an operational calculation, the Scientific Committee should decide whether a missing required series may be omitted or whether it stops the calculation. A missing value should never be omitted without being reported.


3.3 Average across the recent years
The indicator supplied to the harvest rule is:
\[
I = \text{mean of the combined annual indices over the approved recent years}.
\]
For the tested 2027 timing, use 2023–2025 for all CMPs except MP23, which uses 2021–2025. Calculate two versions of (I): an all-index value for MP18, MP23, MP24, MP29, MP31, and MP32, and a CPUE/fishery-index value for MP35 and MP36.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
text:
4 Apply each harvest rule
The table gives the current settings. Catch quantities use the units of the jmMSE analysis; those units and the final rounding convention must be confirmed before providing formal advice.












CMP

Hockeystick
270

For a hockeystick CMP, the preliminary catch advice is the minimum at or below the limit, rises in a straight line between the limit and trigger, and equals the target at or above the trigger.
For MP23, the preliminary advice is (821.875 I) when (I) is above zero.
For MP32 and MP35, let
\[
x = \frac{I-\text{limit}}{\text{trigger}-\text{limit}}.
\]
The preliminary advice is the minimum when (I) is at or below the limit. It is
\[
\text{minimum} + (\text{target}-\text{minimum})x^{2.5}
\]
between the limit and trigger, and
\[
\text{target}(0.2+0.8x)^{0.6}
\]
at or above the trigger. Unlike the hockeystick, the power-ramp advice can continue to rise above its stated target.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
text:
5 Apply the annual change limit
Every CMP in this comparison limits the change from the previous year’s catch advice to a 15% decrease or a 20% increase. If (C^*) is the preliminary advice and (C_{2026}) is the agreed previous advice value, the 2027 value before rounding is
\[
C_{2027} =
\max\left(0.85C_{2026},
\min\left(C^*,1.20C_{2026}\right)\right).
\]
The value 1385 appears as the initial value in the simulation controls. It should not automatically be treated as (C_{2026}). The operational specification must say whether the annual limit is based on the previous TAC, the previous CMP recommendation, or another approved quantity.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
section: 6 Worked retrospective: 2025 advice using data through 2024
text:
6 Worked retrospective: 2025 advice using data through 2024
For this retrospective, the advice year is labelled as the year immediately after the last data year: 2024 observations produce illustrative 2025 advice. This simply backs the calendar labels up one year while retaining the candidate index calculations, harvest rules, and initial comparison value of 1385.
This remains an illustration rather than advice adopted by SPRFMO. The result is a total catch quantity in the analysis units, before an approved rounding rule or allocation among fisheries.

6.1 Historical inputs
The table contains the observations from the updated assessment data associated with input/0.15.dat. The two Peruvian series are retained separately as Peru_Artis and Peru_Ind. Each reference mean is calculated over the available observations in 2019–2023. The northern acoustic series has no 2022 value.

The all-index annual values are 1.141 for 2022, 1.159 for 2023, and 1.013 for 2024. Their three-year mean is 1.104. The corresponding CPUE/fishery-index mean is 1.053. MP23 uses five years and has an indicator of 1.039.

With 1385 as the initial comparison value, a 15% decrease cannot fall below 1177.25. That floor determines the result for seven candidates. MP24 produces 1277.85 before rounding and is not changed by the annual limit.

These values should not be interpreted as eight equally supported recommendations: MP29 and MP32 are the advanced candidates, while the other six are comparison cases. The table is useful for checking that every rule can be reproduced from one shared set of observations.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
section: 7 Why this example stops with 2024 observations
text:
7 Why this example stops with 2024 observations
The updated data contain both Peruvian series through 2025, but the 2025 offshore CPUE is absent. Rather than silently omit that series or invent a replacement, the worked calculation backs off one year and uses the complete set available through 2024. Once the missing-data rule and 2025 offshore CPUE status are settled, the same calculation can be advanced one year to produce an illustrative 2026 result.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
section: 9 What can be concluded from the currently reported value
text:
9 What can be concluded from the currently reported value
The approximately 630 kt acoustic estimate alone is not sufficient to calculate a TAC:

it is outside the tested data window for a 2027 recommendation;
it must be expressed relative to the 2019–2023 average for the same survey;
the other required indices are needed for the all-index CMPs; and
MP35 and MP36 do not use the acoustic survey.

Once the complete annual data table and the agreed previous-advice value are available, the calculations above can be run side by side to show the recommendation produced by every candidate.

title: Illustrative 2027 TAC calculation
text:
10 Implementation basis
This calculation plan is based on the controls saved by the jmMSE candidate runner on branch devel, commit be080e7. The historical index extract used in the worked calculation is saved as cmp-historical-indices.csv. It was extracted from the updated h1_0.16 assessment output associated with input/0.15.dat, retaining both Peruvian index series. The main implementation sources are run_candidate_mps.R and model_tune.R. If the data delays, recent-year windows, combined-index calculation, or annual change rule are revised, this page and the MSE tests must be updated together.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
Slick is an interactive tool for exploring MSE results. The simulation is run in jmMSE; this repository converts the resulting performance table into a Slick file.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
1 Open the current file

1.1 In the hosted app

Download jm_candidates.slick.
Open the Blue Matter Slick app.
If the app has stopped, select Restart app.
Under Load your MSE Results, select the downloaded .slick file.
When the Overview appears, use the MP and OM filters to explore the results.

The hosted app accepts file uploads but does not currently provide a field for loading a file directly from a GitHub URL.


1.2 From R


Show implementation detail
install.packages("Slick") # needed only once
library(Slick)

url <- paste0(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/SPRFMO/jmMSE26/",
  "main/output/jm_candidates.slick"
)
file <- tempfile(fileext = ".slick")
download.file(url, file, mode = "wb")

slick <- readRDS(file)
Check(slick)
App(slick = slick)




1.3 Make it a selectable case study
Slick’s case-study menu is populated from the Blue-Matter/SlickLibrary repository. To request direct integration, copy the file to Slick_Objects/SPRFMO_Jack_Mackerel.slick in a fork of that repository and submit a pull request. Once the SlickLibrary maintainers merge it, SPRFMO Jack Mackerel will appear in the hosted app’s case-study menu. See the official contribution instructions.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
2 What is needed
The conversion can use either:

the table of performance results produced by jmMSE; or
a saved jmMSE results object that already contains those performance results.

The results must include the eight CMPs listed below, the operating-model and stock names, simulation year and replicate, and the calculated performance measures. At a minimum, the results must include spawning biomass relative to \(SB_{MSY}\), fishing mortality relative to \(F_{MSY}\), and catch. Interannual catch change (IACC) is included when available.
The current reference comparison has been completed for eight CMPs with 100 simulation replicates through 2050. It is stored locally in jmMSE as:

model/candidates/reference/runs.rds: the complete simulation object; and
model/candidates/reference/performance.rds: the smaller table used for Slick.

These are reference-operating-model results. They are suitable for checking the candidate calculations and comparing the rule and data contrasts, but they are not a substitute for robustness testing across the full set of operating models.
The nine-OM robustness set has also been completed and is stored locally in jmMSE/model/candidates/robustness/. It contains all eight CMPs, 100 simulation replicates, and annual results through 2050 for every operating model.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
3 CMPs included
MP29 and MP32 remain the two advanced candidates. The other six procedures are comparison cases that help show which part of a procedure causes a change in performance.







CMP
Role in the comparison




MP29
Optimal hockeystick case: target 2000, limit 0.1, all selected indices.


MP32
Alternative powerramp case: target 1500, limit 0.1, all selected indices.


MP18
Extreme high-target case: hockeystick target 5000.


MP24
Low-target case: hockeystick target 1750 and limit 0.


MP31
Higher-limit case: hockeystick target 2000 and limit 0.3.


MP23
Different rule shape: slope HCR with its original five-year averaging window.


MP35
CPUE-only powerramp contrast: target 1500 and limit 0.1.


MP36
CPUE-only hockeystick contrast: target 2000 and limit 0.1.



The MP filter includes one-click selections for the advanced candidates, target contrasts, the limit contrast, rule-shape contrasts, CPUE-only contrasts, all CMPs, and each individual CMP.
MP23 retains the five-year averaging window used in its original tuning code. MP29 and MP32 use three years, so the MP23 comparison changes both rule shape and averaging window; it should not be interpreted as a pure test of shape alone.

title: Load candidates into Slick
section: 4 Run or extend the reference comparison
text:
4 Run or extend the reference comparison
From the jmMSE folder on branch devel:
Rscript run_candidate_mps.R \
  --candidates=tun29,tun32,tun18,tun24,tun31,tun23,tun35,tun36 \
  --mode=reference \
  --cores=1
The candidate codes come from candidate_mps.csv. Additional hockeystick or powerramp trials can be included for contrast, for example:
Rscript run_candidate_mps.R \
  --candidates=tun25,tun29,tun32 \
  --mode=reference \
  --cores=1
The runner uses the recorded definitions in candidate_mps.csv together with the original estimator and rule functions in jmMSE. Shortcut-assessment errors are generated from the original error model using fixed seeds so the comparison can be repeated. Each CMP starts from the same fixed random stream within an operating model, so adding or reordering CMPs does not change the other CMP results. Moving to a newer combined-index implementation should be treated as a method change requiring the operating models to be rebuilt and the candidates to be retuned and revalidated.
The validated reference run used R 4.6.1 with FLCore 2.6.30, FLFishery 0.4.0, mse 2.4.8.9004, msemodules 0.2.2, and FLjjm 0.3.4. The run folder contains the complete sessionInfo.txt record.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
5 Prepare the combined Slick file
From the main jmMSE26 folder:


Show implementation detail
source("R/build_candidate_slick.R")

reference_slick <- build_candidate_slick(
  performance_file = "../jmMSE/model/candidates/reference/performance.rds",
  out_file = NULL,
  historical_om_files = candidate_reference_om_files("../jmMSE")
)


The script organizes the results by:

management procedure (one of the eight CMP comparison cases);
operating model and stock;
simulation replicate and year; and
performance measure, including Kobe-plot values for \(SB/SB_{MSY}\) and \(F/F_{MSY}\).

It also prepares three summary views using the comparison periods already used in jmMSE:

Boxplots show the distribution among simulation replicates in the final year, 2050.
Quilts compare stock status, fishing pressure, catch, and catch stability among operating models and MPs. Long-term results use 2036–2040; short-term catch uses 2026–2030.
Trade-off plots use the same periods to compare conservation, catch, and catch-stability measures directly.

Kobe quadrant probabilities are calculated from \(SB/SB_{MSY}\) and \(F/F_{MSY}\). When \(F_{MSY}\) is zero, \(F/F_{MSY}\) is undefined and is treated as missing. Finite \(F/F_{MSY}\) values above 4 are shown at 4 so that a small number of post-collapse numerical values do not make the plots unreadable. This is a display rule only: the original jmMSE performance tables are not changed.
The OM metadata is kept in exactly the same order as the OM dimension in each Slick value array. This ensures that Northern and Southern stock results retain the correct labels in the two-stock operating models.
Before saving the Slick file, the script checks that the expected dimensions and labels agree. The technical checks are shown in the code for analysts who maintain the export.
The reference is labelled om11 in Slick. Its source name, h1_0.16, remains in the OM metadata for traceability.

5.1 Add the robustness sets
The robustness operating models and candidate runs can be reproduced from jmMSE branch devel with:
Rscript prepare_candidate_robustness_oms.R
Rscript run_candidate_mps.R \
  --candidates=tun29,tun32,tun18,tun24,tun31,tun23,tun35,tun36 \
  --mode=robustness \
  --cores=1
Then build the robustness component and combine it with the reference:


Show implementation detail
source("R/build_candidate_slick.R")

robustness_slick <- build_candidate_slick(
  performance_file = "../jmMSE/model/candidates/robustness/performance.rds",
  out_file = NULL,
  historical_om_files = candidate_robustness_om_files("../jmMSE")
)

slick <- build_combined_candidate_slick(
  reference_slick = reference_slick,
  robustness_slick = robustness_slick,
  out_file = "output/jm_candidates.slick"
)


The resulting Slick file contains 14 OM–stock series: one reference, five single-stock robustness cases, and eight stock-specific series from four two-stock robustness cases. Slick retains the Southern and Northern results rather than averaging them.
The Set filter provides three main categories:

Reference: om11;
Robustness CJM: the five single-stock robustness OMs; and
Robustness 2-stock: the Southern and Northern results from the four two-stock robustness OMs.

The OM and Stock filters remain available for selecting individual models and stock components. One-click presets provide quick selections for the reference, all CJM cases, the CJM robustness cases, the two-stock robustness cases, all OMs, and each individual alternative OM. Selecting a two-stock OM includes both its Southern and Northern rows.
The dimensions are 100 simulations, 14 OM–stock series, eight management procedures, four performance measures, and 81 annual values from 1970 through 2050. Each series uses its own conditioned history through 2025; projections under the eight CMPs begin in 2026.
Do not combine results produced under different package sets without first confirming that the candidate calculations are equivalent.

title: Load candidates into Slick
text:
6 Inspect or launch


Show implementation detail
library(Slick)

slick <- readRDS("output/jm_candidates.slick")
Check(slick)
methods::validObject(slick)

dim(Value(Timeseries(slick)))
Metadata(MPs(slick))
Design(slick)

App(slick = slick)

title: Load candidates into Slick
section: 7 What to save with the Slick file
text:
7 What to save with the Slick file
Save the Slick file together with the original performance results, their digital fingerprint, the exact saved versions of jmMSE and jmMSE26, and the versions of the R packages used.

title: Sources and record of changes
section: 2 Information that must accompany each CMP

title: Sources and record of changes
text:
3 Types of change








Class
Examples
Required review




Wording only
Spelling, navigation, or clearer background explanation
Documentation review


Same calculation, revised code
Software change shown to give the same answers
Code review plus a repeat-calculation test


Scientific
Parameter, index, window, HCR, constraint, allocation, or fallback change
Renewed scientific review and normally MSE evaluation


Status
Test to shortlist, recommendation, adoption, or retirement
Cited decision record

title: Sources and record of changes
text:
4 Work still needed

Confirm the formal working-group status and stable identifiers for MP29 and MP32.
Reconcile the 100-iteration MP29/MP32 tuned triggers with the 500-iteration MP41/MP42 sensitivity values before approving a final version.
Archive the full jmMSE run objects alongside the published Slick file and its digital fingerprint.
Complete the required units, annual cutoffs, missing-data rules, rounding, and exceptional-circumstances provisions.
Add saved worked examples and automatic repeat-calculation checks.

title: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
text:
Start here

CMP registry: MP29 and MP32, plus six clearly labelled comparison cases.
MP29 and MP32: candidate-specific rules and annual calculation details.
Annual application: steps from receiving new data to producing advice.
Slick explorer: compare all eight MSE cases interactively.
How the MSE work fits together: where CMPs fit within the assessment and simulation system.
Evidence and history: current jmMSE evidence with SCW17 development context.
Sources and record of changes: sources, review status, and a record of changes.
crumbs: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
text:
Governing principle
The approved CMP specification is separate from background explanations and MSE results. New scientific results may change whether a candidate is retained, but they do not automatically change its data inputs, harvest control rule, parameter values, catch-allocation method, or exceptional-circumstances rules.
crumbs: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
text:
Terms used on this site







Term
Meaning




CMP
Candidate management procedure: the complete rule for turning agreed data into management advice.


HCR
Harvest control rule: the part of a CMP that converts a stock or index indicator into a catch or fishing-effort recommendation.


Operating model (OM)
A simulated description of the stock and fishery used to test a CMP. Alternative OMs represent different plausible assumptions.


Performance measure
A quantity used to compare CMPs, such as catch, spawning biomass, biological risk, or year-to-year catch variation.


IACC
Interannual catch change: a measure of how much catch changes from one year to the next.
crumbs: SPRFMO Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: Management-procedure components
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
This page summarizes the structure described in SCW17/Paper-03. In jmMSE, a management procedure normally has three parts:
flowchart TD
  D["Observed annual data"] --> E["Calculate a stock or index indicator"]
  E --> S["Indicator used by the rule"]
  S --> H["Harvest control rule: hcr"]
  H --> R["Catch recommendation"]
  R --> I["Apply catch limits and allocation rules"]
  I --> A["Implemented catch by fishery or stock"]

title: Management-procedure components
section: 1 Families represented in the SCW17 inventory
text:
1 Families represented in the SCW17 inventory









Family
Typical estimator
Typical HCR
Role at SCW17




Fixed catch reference
Perfect information
Fixed catch
Diagnostic baseline


Shortcut assessment
Depletion estimate
Hockeystick or buffer
Assessment-based comparison


Empirical index
CPUE/index aggregate
Buffer or hockeystick
Main CMP development family


SPiCT assessment
SPiCT biomass/status
Buffer
Model-based candidate


Multi-index/meta
Multiple index scores
Meta-HCR
Exploratory candidate


Banking/borrowing
Existing estimator/HCR
Existing HCR plus modified isys
Implementation sensitivity



This is a list of approaches that were considered, not a decision about which procedure should be selected. A complete CMP definition must state the exact calculation methods, parameter values, input data, and catch-allocation rules.

title: CMP registry
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: CMP registry
text:
1 Meaning of the status labels







Status
Meaning




Test candidate
Defined sufficiently for testing; not shortlisted or adopted.


Comparison case
Included to isolate an important assumption or design choice; not an advanced or adopted procedure.


Advanced candidate
Selected in the current analysis for robustness testing; formal status not yet confirmed.


Shortlisted
Retained by the working group for formal comparative evaluation.


Recommended
Supported by a documented working-group recommendation.


Adopted
Formally adopted through the applicable SPRFMO process.


Retired
Kept in the historical record but no longer under active consideration.



The primary registry comes from the current approved working version of jmMSE. MP29 and MP32 are the advanced candidates. Six additional CMPs are included as comparison cases in Slick so scientists can quickly examine the effects of target catch, limit, rule shape, and the index set used. The earlier robustness-testing script selected tun29 and tun32, and the 25 July 2026 presentation describes MP29 as a potentially good candidate and MP32 as the optimal powerramp run. They are advanced candidates, not adopted procedures.

title: CMP registry
text:
2 Candidate pages

MP29: empirical all-index estimator plus hockeystick HCR.
MP32: empirical all-index estimator plus power-ramp HCR.
Trial and robustness run reference: the full set of tuning trials and the operating models used to test MP29 and MP32.

The Slick comparison also includes MP18, MP24, MP31, MP23, MP35, and MP36. These are clearly labelled as comparison cases rather than advanced or adopted procedures.

title: CMP registry
text:
3 How these candidates were developed
The jmMSE analysis tested alternative target catches, minimum catches, limit values, harvest control rules, and ways of calculating the stock indicator.








Run
Role in development
July presentation interpretation




MP16
Hockeystick, target 2000, no minimum/limit
Potential candidate before minimum/limit refinement


MP25
Powerramp, target 1500, no minimum/limit
Potential candidate before minimum/limit refinement


MP29
Hockeystick with minimum 270 and limit 0.1
Optimal hockeystick run; advanced to robustness tests


MP32
Powerramp with minimum 270 and limit 0.1
Optimal powerramp run; advanced to robustness tests


MP35-40
CPUE-only, shortcut-assessment, and perfect-information variants
Tests of how the source of stock information affects results


MP41-42
500-replicate versions of the MP29/MP32 designs
Test of whether the number of simulation replicates affects results; not separate CMPs



The earlier SCW17/Paper-09 rows are retained in source history as workshop test proposals. They are not the primary candidate list for this site.

title: CMP registry
text:
4 Points to keep in mind

min, target, and initial are catch quantities in the current code and presentation. lim and trigger are values on the relative-index scale. Final catch units must be stated before adoption.
dlow and dupp limit the decrease and increase in catch advice from one year to the next.
Index names match model_tune.R exactly.
The trigger was tuned so that the probability of being in the Kobe green zone (SB0green: spawning biomass above \(SB_{MSY}\) and fishing mortality below \(F_{MSY}\)) was 0.60 over 2041-2050 in the reference analysis. The resulting trigger is part of the candidate definition and should not be recalculated during annual application.

title: CMP registry
section: 5 Requirements before changing a candidate’s status
text:
5 Requirements before changing a candidate’s status
A candidate can move beyond test status only when it has a completed specification, repeatable performance results, a calculation test using saved inputs, and a decision record identifying the approving body and date.

text:
2 Harvest control rule parameters



Parameter
Value

270

1.42

title: MP32 powerramp candidate
text:
3 Rule
Let \(I_t\) be the annual mean of the standardized index information and
\[
x_t = \frac{I_t-0.1}{1.42-0.1}.
\]
Before applying the annual change limit, the current power-ramp rule calculates the catch recommendation as
\[
C_t^* =
\begin{cases}
270, & I_t \le 0.1,\\
270 + (1500-270)x_t^{2.5}, & 0.1 < I_t < 1.42,\\
1500(0.2+0.8x_t)^{0.6}, & I_t \ge 1.42.
\end{cases}
\]
Unlike a capped hockeystick, the upper branch continues to rise above the trigger. The annual change rule then prevents the result from falling below 85% or rising above 120% of the previous recommendation. For the first application year, the reference value is 1385.

title: MP32 powerramp candidate
text:
4 Evidence status
The July presentation identifies MP32 as the optimal powerramp minimum/limit run. The MP29-MP32 comparison reports broadly similar long-term catch and stock performance, with MP32 showing more year-to-year catch variation (IACC) than MP29. The jmMSE robustness analysis carries both candidates forward.
Primary sources: model_tune.R, model_robustness.R, and the July presentation source.

title: MP32 powerramp candidate
section: 5 Annual application requirements still to resolve

title: CMP specification template
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
Here, normative means that this is the authoritative definition of the CMP: it says what must be done. The template lists the information needed to turn a candidate procedure into a clear and repeatable management rule. Once completed and approved, it identifies the data, calculations, parameter values, catch limits, exceptional-circumstances provisions, and exact calculation method to use. Background explanations and MSE results help the Working Group choose a CMP, but they do not change how it operates. If background text differs from the approved specification, the approved specification takes precedence.
Use this structure for every shortlisted CMP. The approved specification tells analysts how to calculate the advice; supporting evidence belongs in the evidence section.

title: CMP specification template
text:
1 Identity and authority







Field
Required value




CMP identifier
Permanent name or number that is never reused


Version
Version number or date


Status
Test, shortlisted, recommended, adopted, or retired


Effective period
First and last management year, if applicable


Decision record
Body, meeting, document, date, and paragraph


Calculation reference
Repository, exact saved version, file, and function or control name

title: CMP specification template
text:
2 Required input data
For every input state the owner, series name, unit, spatial coverage, seasonal coverage, annual cutoff date, revision policy, missing-value rule, and calculation applied to it. Specify whether preliminary, revised, or final data may be used.

title: CMP specification template
CMP specification template
text:
4 Worked example
Provide a saved set of input data, intermediate values, and final advice. Add a repeat-calculation test or digital fingerprint where useful. A reviewer must be able to reproduce the published answer without relying on data that may later change.

title: CMP specification template
text:
5 Evidence and limitations
Link to MSE runs, operating models, performance metrics, diagnostics, and known limitations. Evidence explains selection but does not redefine the CMP.

title: CMP specification template
text:
6 Change history
For every change, state whether it only improves the wording, changes the code without changing the answer, or changes the scientific rule. A scientific change normally requires renewed MSE evaluation unless the approving decision explicitly states otherwise.

title: Trial and robustness run reference
text:
3 Reading the tables

Rule is the form of the harvest control rule.
Information source identifies the stock information supplied to the rule. all.idx means that all specified abundance indices are combined.
Limit is the indicator value at or below which the minimum catch applies.
Trigger is the indicator value at or above which the target catch applies.
Annual decrease and increase limits are multipliers on the previous year’s catch advice. For example, 0.85 and 1.20 limit a change to a 15% decrease or a 20% increase.
Operating model identifies the alternative description of stock dynamics used for robustness testing. See the MSE analysis for the assumptions that define each operating model.

The detailed definitions of the two advanced candidates are on the MP29 and MP32 pages.

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
text:

Output

Split among fisheries using their average catch proportions over the last five years (split.is)

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
text:
2 Harvest control rule parameters



Parameter
Value




Minimum catch, min
270


Limit metric, lim
0.1


Trigger metric
2.12


Target catch
2000


Initial catch for constraints
1385


Maximum annual decrease multiplier, dlow
0.85


Maximum annual increase multiplier, dupp
1.20



The trigger is the value obtained during tuning and reported in the July presentation. Before formal use it should be copied from the approved CMP record, not recalculated by repeating the tuning exercise.

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
text:
3 Rule
Let \(I_t\) be the annual mean of the standardized index information calculated by the specified method. Before applying the annual change limit, the catch recommendation \(C_t^*\) is
\[
C_t^* =
\begin{cases}
270, & I_t \le 0.1,\\
270 + (I_t-0.1)\dfrac{2000-270}{2.12-0.1}, & 0.1 < I_t < 2.12,\\
2000, & I_t \ge 2.12.
\end{cases}
\]
The annual change rule then prevents the recommendation from falling below 85% or rising above 120% of the previous recommendation. For the first application year, the current calculation uses 1385 as the previous value.

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
text:
4 Evidence status
The July presentation identifies MP29 as the optimal hockeystick minimum/limit run and reports less year-to-year catch variation (IACC) than MP32, with slightly higher short-term catches. The jmMSE robustness analysis carries MP29 and MP32 forward across alternative operating models. Final adoption still requires complete, repeatable robustness results and approved annual data rules.
Primary sources: model_tune.R, model_robustness.R, and the July presentation source.

title: MP29 hockeystick candidate
section: 5 Annual application requirements still to resolve

title: CMP evidence and development history
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures

title: CMP evidence and development history
text:
1 Current candidate evidence from jmMSE
The current jmMSE analysis provides three reasons for carrying MP29 and MP32 forward:

The tuning analysis defines MP29 and MP32 using the same five observed indices, minimum catch, limit, and annual catch-change limits.
The 25 July 2026 presentation identifies MP29 as the optimal hockeystick run, MP32 as the optimal powerramp run, and MP29 as a potentially good candidate after their direct trade-off comparison.
The robustness analysis selects both procedures for testing across the alternative operating models.

The presentation also reports 500-iteration versions, MP41 and MP42, with slightly different retuned triggers. These test whether the number of simulation replicates affects the results; they are not treated as new CMPs.
Before the results support a formal selection decision, they should be repeatable from saved inputs and linked to saved output files that will not be changed.

title: CMP evidence and development history
CMP evidence and development history
CMP evidence and development history

4 Evidence promotion checklist



Check
Required before shortlist
Required before adoption

Desirable

Yes

title: How the MSE work fits together
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
This page condenses SCW17/Paper-04. It shows where this documentation site fits within the jack mackerel assessment and MSE work.
flowchart TD
  J["JJM assessment configurations"] --> C["Condition operating models"]
  C --> O["Operating models with observation and implementation uncertainty"]
  O --> M["Test CMPs in jmMSE"]
  M --> P["Performance metrics and diagnostics"]
  P --> D["CMP comparison and decision"]
  D --> S["Approved written CMP definition in jmMSE26"]
  S --> Y["Apply the CMP when new annual data arrive"]
  Y --> V["Save the data, calculations, and advice"]
crumbs: How the MSE work fits together

title: How the MSE work fits together
text:
1 Separation of responsibilities
jmMSE26 is the documentation and application guide; it is not a separate MSE model. Any calculation examples here must point to an exact saved version of jmMSE. Operating models, recruitment and movement assumptions, reference points, and performance measures remain defined in their scientific source repositories.
crumbs: How the MSE work fits together

title: How the MSE work fits together
text:
2 Reproducibility chain
Every performance claim should identify the operating models, CMP version, number of simulation replicates, treatment of random variation, projection period, performance-measure definition, saved results, and exact analysis version. Every annual application should identify the data revision used and the adopted CMP version.
crumbs: How the MSE work fits together

title: Annual CMP application
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
This workflow describes how an adopted CMP should be applied as new data become available. Trial calculations for candidate CMPs should follow the same steps. For a year-specific example, see the illustrative 2027 TAC calculation.
flowchart TD
  A["Record the approved CMP and software version"] --> B["Receive and save the annual data"]
  B --> C["Check names, units, years, completeness, and revision status"]
  C -->|problem found| X["Stop and document the problem"]
  C -->|checks passed| D["Calculate the stock or index indicator"]
  D --> E["Apply the harvest control rule"]
  E --> F["Apply annual catch limits and allocation rules"]
  F --> G["Check the calculation independently"]
  G -->|results differ| X
  G -->|results agree| H["Review exceptional-circumstances indicators"]
  H --> I["Publish the advice and calculation record"]

title: Annual CMP application
section: 1 1. Record which CMP is being used
text:
1 1. Record which CMP is being used
Record the CMP name and version, the exact saved version of the calculation code, the management year, the analyst and reviewer, and the decision that authorized its use.

title: Annual CMP application
text:
2 2. Save the original data
Keep the files exactly as received. Record who provided them, when they were received, and whether they are preliminary or final. Record a digital fingerprint (checksum) so the exact files can be identified later. If the data must be reformatted, keep the original and document every change.

title: Annual CMP application
text:
3 3. Validate before calculation
Apply the annual data requirements. A failed required check stops the routine calculation. The failure and any subsequent authorized treatment become part of the permanent annual record.

title: Annual CMP application
section: 4 4. Save every important step in the calculation
text:
4 4. Save every important step in the calculation
Save the transformed index values, reference-period values, combined metric, initial HCR recommendation, each annual-limit adjustment, allocation, rounding step, and final advice. Do not save only the final number.

title: Annual CMP application
text:
5 5. Cross-check
Compare against a saved worked example and, where feasible, a calculation made independently. Differences larger than the agreed numerical tolerance must be resolved before advice is released.

title: Annual CMP application
text:
6 6. Exceptional circumstances
Run only the indicators and response process defined in the adopted specification. An exceptional-circumstances review may trigger additional scientific or management consideration; it must not silently rewrite the CMP.

title: Annual CMP application
text:
7 7. Publish the calculation record
The record should contain the input files and their digital fingerprints, data checks, intermediate calculations, final advice, software versions, warnings, analyst and reviewer sign-off, and links to any exceptional decision.

title: Annual input data
text:
South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
    
    Jack Mackerel Candidate Management Procedures
The adopted CMP specification must replace this template with exact series and rules. Until then, this page lists the minimum information that must accompany the data used in each candidate test or trial annual calculation.

title: Annual input data
text:
1 Checks to complete before calculation

File structure: expected columns, names, years, and one value per expected record.
Units: expected scale and any unit conversion.
Completeness: required years and indices are present.
Plausibility: changes outside documented bounds are flagged, not automatically corrected.
Revision status: the source date and whether the data are preliminary or final are recorded.
File identity: the digital fingerprint matches the file named in the calculation record.

Missing or revised data must follow the adopted specification. Analysts should not invent a replacement rule during the annual calculation.
